name: Nathamuni
dates_notes: Sectarian chronicles only; foundational figure who recovered the Āḻvārs' Tamil hymns.
core_thesis: Nāthamuni's surviving works are lost; almost everything attributed to him is reconstructed from later citation. What can be retrieved is a programme rather than a doctrine. The programme has three components. First, the recovery of the Tamil *prabandham* of the *Āḻvārs* — *Tiruvāymoḻi* and the rest — and its placement alongside the Sanskrit *śruti* corpus as authoritative for Vaiṣṇava theology. Second, the integration of Pāñcarātra liturgical theology with *Brahma-Sūtra* exegesis, so that temple-worship of Nārāyaṇa-with-Śrī is treated as continuous with Vedāntic *upāsanā* rather than as a parallel non-Vedic cult. Third, the epistemological claim — preserved chiefly through Yāmuna's citations of the lost *Nyāya-Tattva* — that determinate cognition (*saviśeṣa-jñāna*, awareness with content and structure) is veridical, and that the *jīva* is a real, atomic, knowing self (*jñātṛ*) and not a momentary or undifferentiated *cit*. The constructive Viśiṣṭādvaita position — Brahman *qualified by* the world of *cit* and *acit* — is in Nāthamuni only in seed; its philosophical articulation belongs to Yāmuna and Rāmānuja. What Nāthamuni supplies is the institutional and textual ground on which they build: a bilingual liturgical-philosophical synthesis with the Tamil hymns at one end and Pāñcarātra-Vedānta at the other.

engaged_works.title: Nyaya-Tattva (lost)
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Work is lost; cited in later Śrīvaiṣṇava tradition only.
engaged_works.summary: Lost epistemological treatise reconstructible only from later citations in Yāmuna's *Siddhi-Trayam*, Rāmānuja, and Veṅkaṭanātha. The *Nyāya-Tattva* appears to have been a *prakaraṇa* on *pramāṇa* defending the validity of determinate cognition (*saviśeṣa-jñāna*) and the *jīva* as a substantial, atomic *jñātṛ* against early Buddhist *vijñāna-vāda* and against monist readings of cognition as undifferentiated *cit*. Surviving fragments suggest a critique of the *anyathā-khyāti* and *asat-khyāti* theories of error and an early articulation of *yathārtha-khyāti* (all cognition as veridical) — the position Rāmānuja inherits in the *Vedārtha-Saṃgraha*. The work matters because it is the textual fossil of Viśiṣṭādvaita's epistemological commitment that perception itself is irreducibly relational, that there is no grasping of pure unqualified being, and that this is why Brahman must be construed as *viśiṣṭa* rather than *nirviśeṣa*. Its loss is one of the gravest in Vedānta historiography.

engaged_works.title: Yoga-Rahasya (lost)
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Work is lost; ascription is sectarian.
engaged_works.summary: Lost esoteric manual on *bhakti-yoga* and Pāñcarātra contemplative practice, ascribed to Nāthamuni in Śrīvaiṣṇava sectarian chronicles and quoted occasionally in later Maṇipravāḷa rahasya-literature. The reconstructible content centres on the cultivation of unbroken meditative remembrance of Nārāyaṇa (*dhruvānusmṛti*) as the operative form of *upāsanā* enjoined by the *Upaniṣad*-passages on *vidyā* (e.g., *Chāndogya* 7, *Taittirīya* 2), and on the integration of *aṣṭāṅga-yoga* into a theistic-devotional frame in which the goal is not isolation (*kaivalya*) but conscious participation in the divine. Its significance for the lineage is to ground the later Viśiṣṭādvaita claim — central to Yāmuna and Rāmānuja — that *bhakti* is itself a form of *jñāna*, namely *upāsanā* as enjoined meditative knowledge whose maturation issues directly in *mokṣa*. The Yoga-Rahasya is the missing bridge between Pāñcarātra ritual theology and Vedāntic *upāsanā*-doctrine.
